user_prompt: implement the stuff I said in `lib\AudioManager\readme.md`
let me know if my strategy doesn't make sense
agent_reply: Implemented `lib/AudioManager` as a namespace module with:
- Mic mute/unmute mapped onto the mic FIFOs
- `i2s_channel_register_event_callback` does not exist in this Arduino ESP32 / ESP-IDF 4.4 setup, so I used the legacy I2S event queue from `i2s_driver_install(...)`.
- Core2 internal mic and speaker are not true full-duplex here. The API supports switching speaker/mic mode, but not simultaneous I2S RX/TX on the internal hardware.
- WM8960 is stubbed with a clear log error because the repo does not yet define its pins/codec setup.
Verification: I forced PlatformIO to compile and link `AudioManager` with a temporary check file, then removed that file. Build passed.

user_prompt: hmmm this slipped by me, AudioManager should not be applying the volume to samples, it should just set the volume on the amplifier or DAC, if you don't have enough data on how to do this then just put in a stub, remove both `mono_to_stereo_with_volume` and `apply_volume`
also, make the volume range handled by AudioManager 0 to 30 so we can handle bluetooth 0 to 15 by steps of 2 and generic 10% increments with steps of 3
agent_reply: AudioManager now uses a `0..30` volume range in [AudioManager.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:28). `volumeUp()`/`volumeDown()` step by `3`, and Bluetooth HFP volume maps `0..15` directly to `0..30` by multiplying by `2`.

I removed sample-volume scaling from [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:289). The speaker pump now uses `AudioFifo::dequeueStereo()` directly, so samples are duplicated mono-to-stereo but not gain-adjusted. The old `apply_volume` and `mono_to_stereo_with_volume` helpers are gone.
Hardware volume is now an explicit stub: `update_hardware_volume()`. It just logs for now, which keeps the control path in the right place without pretending we know the Core2 amp/DAC gain route yet.
- Built with a temporary BluetoothManager compile hook: passed.
- Removed the hook and ran normal `pio run`: passed.
- Remaining warnings are the existing unrelated third-party / packed-member warnings.

user_prompt: AudioManager needs to track the mic level simply to show a peaking mic level meter

Inside `pump_mic2bt`, if `i2s_channel_read` is successful, we know how long the chunk is, 16000 samples is one second, and +/- 32767 the maximum possible value. Track the peak value using the absolute value of every sample. Decay it by the appropriate amount such that the decay takes 3 seconds to reach zero. The decay amount is thus calculated from the `bytes_read` variable and also which kind of mic is being used. But also subtract 1 even if nothing is read.
While the peak is tracked using 16 bits, provide a getter method that returns 0 to 100, where 100 is only used to indicate possible clipping.
agent_reply: - `pump_mic2bt()` now updates a 16-bit mic peak whenever `i2s_channel_read()` succeeds.
- SPM1423 uses mono sample count as duration.
- WM8960 uses stereo frame count as duration, while still scanning every sample for peak.
- Peak decays over roughly 3 seconds based on chunk duration.
- If no audio is read, it still decays by `1`.
- Added `AudioManager::micPeakLevel()` in [AudioManager.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h), returning `0..100`; `100` only happens at full-scale `32767`.
I tried `pio run`, but `pio` is still not available on PATH in this shell.

user_prompt: I removed references to WM8960 because it is obsolete, new hardware target is SGTL5000 but I renamed a ton of stuff as external I2S codec
agent_reply: If WM8960 is obsolete, this dependency should be removed or replaced with the SGTL5000/control library you actually plan to use. Right now the build still pulls WM8960-specific code.
That conflicts with the new direction. If WM8960 is no longer valid hardware, this doc should say only SGTL5000 or generic external I2S codec.

Both `enable_exti2scodec_speaker()` and `enable_exti2scodec_mic()` return `false`, so selecting `Hardware::ExternalI2SCodec` cannot currently enable speaker or mic mode. If the rename was meant to preserve behavior while swapping codec hardware, this is the main functional gap.
4. [AudioManager.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:553) assumes every external I2S codec mic stream is stereo.

That matches what you described, but it should be considered part of the external codec contract. If SGTL5000 is ever configured mono at the codec or I2S slot level, this path will incorrectly halve the frame count and mix adjacent mono samples as L/R.

The broad rename to `ExternalI2SCodec` looks consistent in `AudioManager.h/.cpp`; I don’t see leftover `WM8960` identifiers in that module. The stale references are mainly dependency/docs, plus the external codec setup stubs. I couldn’t use `git diff` because this shell reported the directory as not a git repo.

user_prompt: I want to develop a microphone gain manager, using `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.cpp` and `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.h`. There only needs to be one instance. The microhpone stream is assumed to be mono. The bit depth is always 16 bit PCM just like the rest of the project. The sample rate does not matter.

There should be a public facing initialization function and a "process" function that acts in-place on a buffer. There is a privately kept set of state variables. Also publically there are some getters and setters as needed.
All functionality related to tracking mic peak will now be moved to `MicGainManager`
The initialization will set an initial mic gain of 1.5x, note that a test showed that actually screaming into the mic results in about 75% full scale peak.

There is a target-gain, a current-gain, and a previous-gain. The current-gain can go up or go down once per call of process. The rate it goes up is slower than the rate it can go down (it's more important to respond to clipping).

The difference between current-gain and previous-gain is that, at the begining of "process", it uses previous-gain until a zero-cross event, then it uses current-gain and updates previous-gain. And then calculate the current-gain according to target-gain.
The mic peak is tracked using the raw unmodified values. The peak being tracked decays using the same method we use now, but called from within "process".
The target-gain is continously set to keep the mic peak at about 75% of full scale. It is clamped between 1x and 3x.
The mic peak is publicly readable so the GUI can display the peak, the mic peak after scaling with the current setting is also publicly available.
There is also to be a fixed gain mode, for when users manually control the gain. And there is to be a bypass mode.

The way the sample manipulation should work is: cast the sample to 32 bit, the 32 bit sample is multiplied by a gain, and then divided by a divisor, then written back into the original buffer as 16 bit. The gain scale is thus controlled by the divisor. We can start with a divisor of 128, and so the gain step of 1 is achievable and if my calculations are correct:

Sample rate is 16000 Hz, the I2S read buffer is set to 240 samples, so there are 66.67 calls of "process" per second. If we use 128 as the divisor, and want to double the gain automatically as slow as possible, we can go from 128 to 256 in about 2 seconds. And using 128, a power of 2, gives the compiler a chance to use bit shifting for the division. And 128 is decent resolution such that humans cannot detect any jarring transitions.
Warn me if my logic is flawed.
agent_reply: - Implemented in-place 16-bit mono PCM processing in [MicGainManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/MicGainManager.cpp:184).
- Moved mic peak tracking out of `AudioManager`.
- Added auto gain, fixed gain mode, bypass mode, raw peak, and scaled peak accessors.
- Wired `AudioManager::pump_mic2bt()` to process mic samples before queueing them in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:643).
Your divisor math is good: `240` samples at `16000 Hz` is `66.67` process calls/sec, so `128` one-unit gain steps takes about `1.92s`.
- Initial `1.5x` gain means a raw `75%` full-scale scream can clip at the output before AGC pulls down.
- During real active silence, auto gain will drift toward `3x`; that can make the first loud transient hot. I prevented no-sample/idling calls from ramping gain.
- Zero-cross-only gain switching can delay gain changes if the mic signal has DC offset or very few crossings. A timeout fallback may be worth adding later.
Small note: `pio run` regenerated the auto-generated `src/version.c` build nonce.

agent_reply: Added `audio diag mic` logging and the underlying counters.
- mic pump calls and skip reasons: not mic mode / no I2S / FIFO full
- I2S read calls, requested/read bytes, empty reads, read errors, raw samples
- notify condition checks, ready count, actual notify calls
- failure buckets for notify: no queued data / below minimum / Bluetooth not ready
- current mic FIFO readable samples, notify minimum, and current `can_bt`
On the next log, the key read is:
If `call` increments but `audio diag out cb` stays `0`, then we are notifying Bluedroid but it still is not fetching outgoing audio.

user_prompt: apply the fix for the full fifo case, but also, letting the mic2bt fifo fill up is a problem. Queuing mic2file should be allowed but queuing mic2bt should be choked unless we are certain that Bluetooth audio is ready to push into
agent_reply: - `mic2file` keeps getting queued whenever mic samples are read.
- `mic2bt` is cleared and not queued while `BtManager::canNotifyOutgoingAudioReady()` is false.
- If Bluetooth is ready but `mic2bt` is full, `pump_mic2bt()` now attempts `notifyOutgoingAudioReady()` instead of returning early.
- `mic2bt` only receives new mic samples when Bluetooth is ready and the FIFO has write space.
- `bt_drop notready/full=.../...` was added to `audio diag mic`.
- `notready` counts mic samples intentionally not queued to Bluetooth because HFP audio was not ready.
- `full` counts mic samples that could not fit into `mic2bt` while Bluetooth was ready.
In the next log, the important thing is whether `q_bt` starts increasing only after `can_bt=1`, and whether `notify ... call` increments.

user_prompt: how is `HFP_AUDIO_DIAG` being enabled for being compiled in?
agent_reply: `HFP_AUDIO_DIAG` is compiled in only when `ENABLE_HFP_AUDIO_DIAGNOSTICS` is defined.
So if `ENABLE_HFP_AUDIO_DIAGNOSTICS` exists in the build flags before `AudioManager.cpp` compiles, the diagnostic counters are live. If not, every `HFP_AUDIO_DIAG(...)` call becomes a no-op.
Where it likely gets enabled: PlatformIO build flags. Search result says it appears in the code, but I’d check `platformio.ini` / extra scripts for something like:
or a conditional build script adding that define.

The related structs/functions in [AudioManager.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:36) are also behind the same macro, so callers only see `HfpAudioDiagnostics`, `hfpAudioDiagnostics()`, and `resetHfpAudioDiagnostics()` when that flag is defined.

user_prompt: add a calling argument for sample rate
if the argument is 0, use the default of 16000, or make the argument optional and default it to 16000
when starting MP3 playback, use the appropriate sample rate from the file header
agent_reply: `AudioManager::enableSpeakerMode()` now takes an optional sample rate:
Internally, `enable_ns4168_speaker()` and `enable_exti2scodec_speaker()` also accept the sample rate. Passing `0` falls back to `16000`.

For playback, I added a `FilePlayback::speakerSampleRateHz()` hook. Default playback still returns `16000`, while `Mp3Playback` overrides it to return the parsed MP3 header sample rate. `FilePlayback::setupSpeaker()` now calls:
So MP3 playback starts the I2S speaker at the MP3 file’s actual sample rate.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`, build passes.

commit_message: mp3 playback attempt to support other formats

user_prompt: "Under state `EXTCODEC_NO_EARBUD` we still have push-to-talk behavior, but instead of switching to the `SPM1423` when we push-to-talk, we switch to the `SGTL5000` using line-in-right as the sound source.",

"Under `EXTCODEC_YES_EARBUD` we have bidirectional audio, the mic is using line-in-right, do not use push-to-talk behavior, we can mute the mic, we can mute the sound output, but now changing mic state doesn’t change sound output state, and changing sound output state doesn’t change mic state",

"Under `EXTCODEC_YES_EARBUD_WITH_MIC` we have bidirectional audio, the mic is using the `SGTL5000`'s dedicated mic input, do not use push-to-talk behavior, we can mute the mic, we can mute the sound output, but now changing mic state doesn’t change sound output state, and changing sound output state doesn’t change mic state",
enable_exti2scodec_speaker and enable_exti2scodec_mic should probably become the same thing now? and they don't duplicate configureAnalogPathForState?
agent_reply: Implemented the shared external codec path.

The key change is in [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:921): `enable_exti2scodec_speaker` and `enable_exti2scodec_mic` are gone in favor of one `enable_exti2scodec(...)` path, with a new `P2TMode::FullDuplex`. The SGTL5000 I2S setup now opens TX/RX together once, and speaker-only, mic-only, and full-duplex are just mode choices on top of that.
- no earbud: stays push-to-talk, but mic mode uses the external codec path
- earbud / earbud+mic: enters full duplex, mic mute and speaker mute stay independent
- mock state changes re-sync routing through `AudioManager::syncExternalCodecRouting()` instead of duplicating `configureAnalogPathForState()`

Startup now selects `AudioManager::Hardware::ExternalI2SCodec` when `ExtCodec::available()` is true in [main.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:181), so the mock can actually drive the external-codec behavior.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0` successfully. `git diff --check` also exits cleanly, with only the existing CRLF warnings.
Implemented the shared external codec path.

The key change is in [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:921): `enable_exti2scodec_speaker` and `enable_exti2scodec_mic` are gone in favor of one `enable_exti2scodec(...)` path, with a new `P2TMode::FullDuplex`. The SGTL5000 I2S setup now opens TX/RX together once, and speaker-only, mic-only, and full-duplex are just mode choices on top of that.
- no earbud: stays push-to-talk, but mic mode uses the external codec path
- earbud / earbud+mic: enters full duplex, mic mute and speaker mute stay independent
- mock state changes re-sync routing through `AudioManager::syncExternalCodecRouting()` instead of duplicating `configureAnalogPathForState()`

Startup now selects `AudioManager::Hardware::ExternalI2SCodec` when `ExtCodec::available()` is true in [main.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:181), so the mock can actually drive the external-codec behavior.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0` successfully. `git diff --check` also exits cleanly, with only the existing CRLF warnings.

user_prompt: ok that is fine, I already routed out the MCLK signal in the PCB design
so then what is the resulting MCLK frequency that the ESP32 is outputting, and then what is the multiplier that the SGTL5000 is using?
agent_reply: and the default sample rate is [AudioManager.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:78):
- `RATE_MODE = 1/2`, so actual audio sample rate is `16 kHz`
So the SGTL5000 sees the same thing two ways:
- Relative to the real audio LRCLK: `MCLK = 512 * 16 kHz = 8.192 MHz`
Also, current `CHIP_I2S_CTRL = 0x0130`, so BCLK is `32 * Fs = 512 kHz` for 16 kHz 16-bit stereo.

user_prompt: do a build, fix the linker error
agent_reply: Build reproduced the linker error and it’s fixed now.

The issue was that `AudioManager.h` declared public `AudioManager::configure_i2s_shared()`, but `AudioManager.cpp` only defined a same-named function inside an anonymous namespace, so the public symbol was never emitted. I renamed the hidden implementation to `configure_i2s_shared_impl()` and added the public wrapper in [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:804).
Validation: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` now succeeds. I restored `src/version.c` to its pre-build state; it was already dirty when I started, so I left that existing change alone.

user_prompt: ok we need to move MCLK startup to `void all_init()`, I think I want the entire `AudioManager::configure_i2s_shared()` to be called there. I have made this edit
switching between NS4168 should not cause a I2S reconfiguration. I have edited the code to do this
fix any mistakes and missing includes
agent_reply: Done. I kept your direction and fixed the rough edges:
- Added the direct `AudioManager.h` include and kept `AudioManager::configure_i2s_shared()` in `all_init()` at [src/all_init.cpp](<d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:5>).

- Made shared I2S an explicit tracked state in [AudioManager.cpp](<d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:71>), so NS4168 and SGTL5000 paths can reuse it instead of inferring from codec availability.
- Changed NS4168 and SGTL5000 enable paths to call “ensure shared I2S” rather than blindly stopping/recreating the bus.
- Changed `AudioManager::stop()` to stop the logical audio mode while keeping shared I2S/MCLK alive; if internal PDM was active, it tears that down and restores shared I2S.
- Capped the I2S TX credit counter so always-on I2S at boot cannot accumulate bogus write credit forever.
It builds successfully. `src/version.c` was restored after PlatformIO regenerated it.

user_prompt: I have 5 of these PCBs made with the external codec, they simply plug into the bottom of the M5Stack Core2 running our code. These PCBs all fully work, but there's a noise in the microphone that is amplified by a MAX4466 routed to the line-in-right of the SGTL5000. So these 5 different PCBs each have a different small modification, such as an additional capacitor soldered to a different location, and I want to evaluate how effective each modification is at removing the unwanted noise.
I've been trying to do this by simply recording the WAV files but this is extremely slow. So I want a on-device visualization tool.
The function `test_noisespectrumanalyzer` is a core-1 function. It should spawn a core-0 thread. Following our convention, core-1 is for GUI tasks and core-0 is for audio tasks.
During initialization, initialize `ExtCodec` and `AudioManager` and the LCD screen, we do not really need the whole GUI, just the screen. We will want to use a full screen canvas (M5Canvas).
The audio input should be set to use the line-in-right microphone no matter what.

The core-0 thread is simply pumping in audio from I2S (reading I2S while enqueuing FIFO). Core-1 thread is doing analysis (dequeuing from FIFO), one analysis for one full screen update, but also rate capped at 30FPS.

The FFT for the spectrum analysis is basically... use a 512 point FFT, bin spacing 31.25 Hz, the bottom of the screen is 320 pixels, but use 256 pixels of it, from the left edge, for 256 frequency bins. For vertical scaling, there's 240 pixels available, we will use two different scales
First scale: pick a fixed scaling that makes sense assuming 10% of full scale is the maximum a raw sample could be. We might adjust this.
Second scale: basically a full scale that can fit the maximum magnitude expected.

Use vertical lines for the bars (use white for the small scale, use green for the full scale). If the user touches the screen, then the touch coordinate selects a particular frequency bin, if one is selected, draw it with orange instead.
If the user touches off of the graph area, then deselect the frequency. If the user pressed BtnA or BtnC, then select the left or right adjacent frequency bin relative to the currently selected one.

So this there's about 64 pixels blank on the right side of the screen. Use the smallest font, display the RMS power, and if selected, display the frequency selected and the magnitude at that frequency. While the GUI update is rate limited to 30FPS, rate limit the text updates to 5 per second so it doesn't become a blur.
Remember to use M5Canvas, draw the graph before drawing the text, draw the text right-justified and with a black background so it can overflow into the graph if needed.

Everything is done with best-effort performance. I don't care if packets are dropped, as I am not listening to the sound, I just want to visualize. So use the latest 512 samples in the FIFO always, the rest can be discarded.
Using any built-in DSP helpers is optional, if it is easy then go ahead, if it requires importing libraries or editing `platformio.ini` then avoid it.
As this is a self contained test, try not touching other code files unless absolutely necessary.
agent_reply: Implemented `test_noisespectrumanalyzer()` in [src/tests/test_noisespectrumanalyzer.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_noisespectrumanalyzer.cpp:484). It initializes the LCD/canvas, external codec, and AudioManager, forces SGTL5000 line-in-right, spawns a core-0 audio pump, keeps the newest 512 samples, runs a 512-point FFT, and renders 256 bins with 10%/100% scale toggle, touch bin selection, adjacent-bin buttons, RMS/bin readout, and 5 Hz text refresh.

I added a tiny AudioManager override hook at [AudioManager.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:98) so the test can force line-in-right even if the codec detect state would normally choose the earbud mic.
Build passed. I left the pre-existing working-tree changes/untracked files outside my edit scope alone.

user_prompt: I want to implement a test that tests hardware functionality really quickly
Create a new view inheriting from `FlyGuiView` as `lib\FlyGuiViews\MasterTestView.cpp` and `lib\FlyGuiViews\MasterTestView.h`
when the test is enabled, at the bottom of `void setup()` where it shows the main view, if the test is enabled, then transition into the MasterTestView
At this point, the splash screen is already shown and would've shown the microSD card info, the clock should be working, the battery status is being shown too, so that's a lot that's been tested
The `MasterTestView` will be basically a 3x2 grid. I won't be using sprites, just text centered on the buttons will be enough
For the top row, the top left square is reserved for a display of the ADC values and `EXTCODEC_*` `State` value. This is not clickable
The top middle will be a toggle with the text "earbud", touching it activates or deactivates it, draw a red box around it when activated. When activated, play a sine tone to the external codec.
The top right will be a toggle with the text "speaker", touching it activates or deactivates it, draw a red box around it when activated. When activated, play a sine tone to the internal speaker.
Make these mutually exclusive. There needs to be an override that allows for the internal speaker to be used even though an earbud is available.

The bottom left square is supposed to visualize mic sound. Keep a buffer of 100 values, the mic peak values. On each refresh of the screen, read the latest mic peak value, shift the entire buffer discarding the oldest value, put the newest value at the other end, and then render each index as vertical bar height

The bottom middle button will be a toggle with the text "earbud\nmic", touching it activates or deactivates it, draw a red box around it when activated. When activated, the mic source switches to the inline-mic of the earbud.

The bottom right button will be a toggle with the text "internal\nmic", touching it activates or deactivates it, draw a red box around it when activated. When activated, the mic source switches to the internal line-in mic.
again, there might need to be an override to force the usage of a particular mic disregarding the actual state of the `EXTCODEC_*` `State`.
touching anything will cause the same haptics as normal GUI interactions.

the clock should be ticking away as all of this happens. the battery indicator should be live updated as usual. `Hotel` should also be working. Hell I think basically not showing the `MainScreenView` but letting everything else run as if it wasn't a test is just fine.
remember to follow the code layout policy at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`.
